elements.sent-2411-001: Super-resolution (SR) is a key technique for improving the visual quality of video content by increasing its spatial resolution while reconstructing fine details.
elements.sent-2411-002: SR has been employed in many applications including video streaming, where compressed low-resolution content is typically transmitted to end users and then reconstructed with a higher resolution and enhanced quality.
elements.sent-2411-003: To support real-time playback, it is important to implement fast SR models while preserving reconstruction quality; however most existing solutions, in particular those based on complex deep neural networks, fail to do so.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this issue, this paper proposes a low-complexity SR method, RTSR, designed to enhance the visual quality of compressed video content, focusing on resolution up-scaling from a) 360p to 1080p and from b) 540p to 4K.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proposed approach utilizes a CNN-based network architecture, which was optimized for AV1 (SVT)-encoded content at various quantization levels based on a dual-teacher knowledge distillation method.
elements.sent-2411-006: It achieved the best trade-off between complexity and coding performance (measured in PSNR, SSIM and VMAF) among all six submissions.
elements.sent-2411-007: The code will be available soon.
elements.sent-2411-008: Visual content has become increasingly prominent in today's digital landscape \cite{bull2021intelligent .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The widespread use of digital devices has led to a significant increase in the consumption of video content across various applications such as live streaming, broadcasting, video conferencing, and surveillance.
elements.sent-2411-010: These video-centric applications now represent a substantial portion, approximately 80\%, of global internet traffic \cite{r:cisco2 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{scatter.pdf} \caption{Scatter plot for six methods (\ref{sec:com-model}) in terms of the balance between VMAF and PSNR-Y performance and MACs/pixel. The upper left indicates a better performance-complexity trade-off for the corresponding method.} \label{fig:radar-chart}
elements.sent-2411-011: Video compression is one of the most widely researched topics in image and video processing.
elements.sent-2411-012: It plays a crucial role in the trade-off between the (substantial) bit rate required for transmitting immersive, high-quality video content and the limited network bandwidth available.
elements.sent-2411-013: Over the past few decades, the efficiency of video codecs has seen notable advances, with the development of video coding standards including H.266/VVC \cite{VVCSoftware_VTM and AOM AV1 \cite{AV1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: These standards are, however, all based on the same rate-distortion optimization framework as their predecessors (e.g., HEVC \cite{h265HEVC and VP9 \cite{VP9 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-015: As such they may not be able to fulfill the significantly growing demand associated with emerging media formats, in particular when higher spatial resolutions are adopted.
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, inspired by advances in deep learning, numerous learning-based video coding methods have emerged \cite{dvc, kwan2023hinerv, mimt, canfvc, kwan2024nvrc, li2024neural .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Among these solutions, an important class focuses on the use of deep neural networks to enhance individual coding tools within conventional coding architectures \cite{yan2018convolutional, zhang2020enhancing, ma2020cvegan, ma2020mfrnet, ViSTRA3, li2024video, huo2024towards, ibraheem2024enhancing .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although these approaches have shown significant promise, delivering evident coding gains, they are also associated with high complexities, with slow run-times and large memory requirements.
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, for many of these methods, simple distortion-based loss functions, e.g., mean squared error (MSE) and L1, are utilized in the training process, despite their relatively poor correlation with perceived visual quality.
elements.sent-2411-020: As a result, they often fail to produce results with optimal perceptual quality \cite{ma2020cvegan .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this context, this paper describes a low-complexity super-resolution approach, RTSR, specifically optimized for AV1 compressed content.
elements.sent-2411-022: Inspired by the EDSR block \cite{lim2017enhanced , this method is designed to up-scale 360p compressed videos to 1080p resolution and 540p to 4K in real-time.}
elements.sent-2411-023: To further enhance super-resolution performance, a knowledge distillation strategy, proposed in \cite{jiang2023compressing, jiang2024mtkd has been applied; a perceptually-inspired Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) architecture, CVEGAN \cite{ma2020cvegan and EDSR\_baseline \cite{lim2017enhanced were used as dual-teacher models.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, a perceptually-inspired loss function, developed in \cite{ma2020cvegan ), is employed in the training and optimization processes in order to produce results with improved perceptual video quality.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This approach has been integrated within the SVT-AV1 version 1.8.0 video codec and was submitted to the Grand Challenge in Real Time Video Super-Resolution (ECCV 2024 AIM (Advances in Image Manipulation) Workshop (Team BVI-RTVSR)) \cite{conde2024aim .}
elements.sent-2411-026: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:radar-chart , RTSR offers the best trade-off between complexity and performance - it exceeds the Pareto front of the other five solutions in three of four cases with different quality metrics (PSNR and VMAF) and complexity combinations.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-028: Section \ref{sec:PA describes the proposed RTSR method, the integrated coding framework, and the training process.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The experiment setup is detailed in Section \ref{sec:E .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The coding results are then analyzed in Section \ref{sec:RD .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Finally, Section \ref{sec:c concludes the paper and outlines future works.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The proposed coding framework is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:coding_frame .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Prior to encoding, the original YUV input 1080p video is downsampled by a factor of 3 or 4 using a Lanczos filter.
elements.sent-2411-034: SVT-AV1 v1.8.0 \cite{AV1 serves as the Host Encoder used to compress low-resolution videos.}
elements.sent-2411-035: At the decoder, the low-resolution video stream is decoded and the proposed RTSR approach is applied to reconstruct the full resolution video content while enhancing its perceptual quality.
elements.sent-2411-036: Details of the network architecture and the training process are described below.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Picture1.pdf} \caption{ The proposed coding framework, with an RTSR module.} \label{fig:coding_frame}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Picture2.pdf} \caption{ The network structure of the proposed RTSR model. Factor $s$ represents the upsampling factor. Here block number B is set to 3, and channel number C is 24.} \label{fig:network}
elements.sent-2411-037: The network architecture, shown in Figure \ref{fig:network , takes a 48$\times$48 YCbCr 4:2:0 compressed image block as input, which is first up-sampled using a nearest neighbor (NN) filter to 48$\times$48 YCbCr 4:4:4 \cite{ma2020cvegan .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The output is a 144$\times$144 image block (for the $\times$3 up-scaling) in YCbCr 4:4:4 format, which is then converted into YCbCr 4:2:0 format, targeting its original uncompressed full resolution version.
elements.sent-2411-039: For the $\times$4 up-scaling, a factor of 4 is used in the final PixelShuffle layer, producing a 192$\times$192 image block as output.
elements.sent-2411-040: Knowledge distillation (KD) has emerged as a promising technique for improving the performance of low-complexity super-resolution networks \cite{jiang2024mtkd, he2020fakd .}
elements.sent-2411-041: We use this approach in the proposed RTSR model which learns from two teacher models: a perceptually-inspired network for compressed video enhancement, CVEGAN\cite{ma2020cvegan , and the EDSR\_baseline \cite{lim2017enhanced .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: In order to meet real-time requirements, a PixelunShuffle layer is also used before convolutional layers to greatly reduce the number of operations.
elements.sent-2411-043: The main body of the network consists of B identical blocks, each with a ReLU layer and two consecutive convolutional layers, which are highlighted in Figure \ref{fig:network .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The up-sampling module (after B convolutional blocks) consists of a two-step PixelShuffle operation.
elements.sent-2411-045: UV channels are up-sampled by a bicubic filter and concatenated with the Y-channel recovered by the CNN model.
elements.sent-2411-046: The training of the proposed model is performed in two stages, described in Algorithm \ref{alg:training .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the first stage, the student model is trained from scratch, with a combined perceptual loss function, proposed in \cite{ma2020cvegan . \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{p} = 0.3 \mathcal{L}_\mathit{L1} + 0.2 \mathcal{L}_\mathit{SSIM} + 0.1 \mathcal{L}_\mathit{L2} + 0.4 \mathcal{L}_\mathit{MS-SSIM}\end{equation} In the same stage, two teacher models, CVEGAN and EDSR\_baseline, are also trained from scratch with identical training content.}
elements.sent-2411-048: We followed the training configurations described in the source publications \cite{ma2020cvegan, lim2017enhanced .}
elements.sent-2411-049: In the second stage, the pre-trained RTSR model is used as the student model, which learns from its pre-trained teachers, CVEGAN and EDSR\_baseline using the following loss function: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\mathit{total} = \alpha\mathcal{L}_\mathit{Lap}(I_\mathit{stu}, I_\mathit{gt}) + \Sigma \mathcal{L}_\mathit{Lap}(I_\mathit{stu}, I_\mathit{tchr}), \label{Ltotal}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{L _{Lap (I_{stu , I_{gt )$ denotes the original loss between the ground truth $I_{gt}$ and the student model’s prediction $I_{stu}$, and $\alpha$ is a tunable weight.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: We have tested several commonly used loss functions, including $\mathit{L1}$, $\mathit{L2}$, $\mathit{MSE}$, $\mathit{SSIM}$, $\mathit{MS-SSIM}$, and found that Laplacian loss performs the best in terms of various quality metrics including PSNR and VMAF.
elements.sent-2411-052: The RTSR implementation is based on PyTorch version 1.10 \cite{paszke2019pytorch .}
elements.sent-2411-053: We used the following configurations during training: Adam optimization \cite{kingma2014adam with the hyper-parameters: $\beta_{1 = 0.9$ and $\beta_{2 = 0.999$; batch size of 16; 200 training epochs (100 for both stage 1 and 2); initial learning rate of $10^{-4}$; weight decay of 0.1 for every 50 epochs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The hyperparameter $\alpha$ in Eq. (\ref{Ltotal ) is set to 0.1, following \cite{morris2023st .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The block number B is 3, and channel number C is 24, which results in a complexity below 2K MACs/pixel.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \SetKwInOut{Input}{Input} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} \SetAlgoLined $ contain cropped original sequences $y$ and compressed sequences pairs $x$} =\{31,39,47,55,63\}$} =\{\times3,\times4\}$} \Output{Trained student model $S_{\times F}$.} \BlankLine \For{$F \in \mathcal{F}$}{ Stage1: Train CVEGAN $T_1$, EDSR\_baseline $T_2$, RTSR $S_{\times F}$ separately with loss function $\mathcal{L}_{p}$ from scratch. Stage2: Transfer knowledge from $T_1$ and $T_2$ to $S_{\times F}$. \ForEach{batch of data $(x, y)$ in $D$}{ Compute teacher1 predictions: $z_{T_1} = T(x)$\; Compute teacher2 predictions: $z_{T_2} = T(x)$\; Compute student predictions: $z_S = S_{\times F}(x)$\; Compute distillation loss: $\Sigma \mathcal{L}_\mathit{Lap}(I_\mathit{z_S}, I_\mathit{z_{T_i}})$\; Compute standard loss: $\mathcal{L}_\mathit{Lap}(I_\mathit{z_S}, I_\mathit{y})$\; Compute total loss: $\mathcal{L}_\mathit{total}$\; Update student model $S_{\times F}$ using gradient descent\; } } \caption{The training strategy used for optimization RTSR.} \label{alg:training}
elements.sent-2411-056: The RTSR, CVEGAN, and EDSR\_baseline models were optimized using the training database described in Sec.. \ref{sec:Exp-data .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In addition to the LDV3 videos provided as part of the AIM Grand Challenge \cite{conde2024aim , original sequences from the BVI-DVC database \cite{ma2021bvi and the BVI-AOM database \cite{nawala2024bvi were also employed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: BVI-DVC contains 800 diverse uncompressed video sequences at various resolutions, and has been employed as a training database for MPEG JVET to optimize neural network-based coding tools for VVC, while BVI-AOM includes more content with complex structures (e.g., fire, water, or plasma) and artistic intent (e.g., action movie like face close-ups).
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{minipage}{0.495\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.98\linewidth]{Chimera-WindAndNature.jpg}} \text{Chimera-WindAndNature} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.495\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.98\linewidth]{NETFLIXElFuente.jpg}} \text{NETFLIXElFuente} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.495\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.98\linewidth]{NETFLIXElFuente90s.jpg}} \text{NETFLIXElFuente90s} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.495\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.98\linewidth]{Sparks-Sunrise.jpg}} \text{Sparks-Sunrise} \end{minipage} \caption{Frame samples from the high-quality test set videos. The original videos are in 4K resolution YCbCr 4:2:0 format.} \label{fig:testsetexample}
elements.sent-2411-059: All the original sequences collected were encoded using SVT-AV1 version 1.8.0, with five quantization parameter (QP) values (31, 39, 47, 55, and 63).
elements.sent-2411-060: Subsequently, both the compressed sequences and their original counterparts were cropped into 48$\times$48 and 144$\times$144 patches (192$\times$192 for the $\times$4 up-scaling), respectively, and randomly selected for training.
elements.sent-2411-061: Data augmentation techniques such as rotation and flipping were applied to increase content diversity.
elements.sent-2411-062: This results in a total of 92,800 pairs of patches.
elements.sent-2411-063: Based on all the generated training material, we trained a single CNN model for compressed video content with various QPs (for each $\times$3 or $\times$4 up-scaling).
elements.sent-2411-064: Nineteen downsampled (using the Lanczos5 filter) sequences, provided by the AIM challenge organizers, have been used to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed coding framework.
elements.sent-2411-065: Each sequence contains up to 1799 frames and is compressed with five different QPs, ranging from 31 to 63, after down-sampling.
elements.sent-2411-066: The decoded sequences were also provided by the organizer in mp4 format.
elements.sent-2411-067: Several sample frames are shown in Figure \ref{fig:testsetexample .}
elements.sent-2411-068: For evaluation, the compressed low-resolution sequences were first converted to the YCbCr 4:4:4 format.
elements.sent-2411-069: The RTSR model processes 16 consecutive low-resolution frames each time.
elements.sent-2411-070: Quality metrics PSNR, SSIM, and VMAF \cite{VMAF are employed to measure video quality.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In addition, model complexity figures, including \#Params(M), MACs(G) and Runtime (ms), are also measured using VideoAI \cite{VideoAI and reported here.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Further information about the AIM 2024 Grand Challenge can be found in \cite{conde2024aim .}
elements.sent-2411-073: The proposed method has been benchmarked against eight reference methods, including EDSR\_baseline \cite{lim2017enhanced , CVEGAN \cite{ma2020cvegan , Lanczos5 filter, SuperBicubic++, FSMD, ETDSv2, VPEG-VSR, SAFMN++ \cite{conde2024aim .}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The results of the latter six were provided by the AIM 2024 Grand Challenge organizers.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Efficient VSR Challenge Benchmark.} \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c|c|c|c} Method PSNR-Y $\uparrow$ SSIM-Y $\uparrow$ VMAF $\uparrow$ \# Params (M) $\downarrow$ MACs/pixel (K) $\downarrow$ Runtime (ms) $\downarrow$ \multicolumn{7}{c}{Track 1: Mobile, 360p to 1080p ($\times$3)} Anchor(Lanczos5) 33.123 0.9364 51.241 - - - SuperBicubic++ 30.513 0.9250 66.389 0.05 1.40 0.46 (A100) FSMD 32.808 0.9384 60.166 1.624 45.18 13.14 (4090) ETDSv2 32.205 0.9333 48.127 0.136 17.15 8.6 (A100) VPEG-VSR 28.836 0.8635 34.442 0.070 7.81 3.84 (3090) \cellcolor{green1}RTSR (ours) \cellcolor{green1}33.329 \cellcolor{green1}0.9371 \cellcolor{green1}55.438 \cellcolor{green1}0.062 \cellcolor{green1}1.89 \cellcolor{green1}0.81 (3090) \multicolumn{7}{c}{Track 2: General, 540p to 4K ($\times$4)} Anchor(Lanczos5) 34.651 0.9577 46.049 - - - SuperBicubic++ 30.572 0.9416 64.112 0.398 24.92 10.77 (A100) FSMD 34.329 0.9591 55.920 1.599 25.02 32.33 (4090) ETDSv2 33.734 0.9564 43.339 0.136 4.29 8.6 (A100) VPEG-VSR 31.568 0.9111 36.704 0.077 2.41 8.56 (3090) SAFMN++ 29.294 0.8774 29.225 0.040 1.23 8.2 (3090) \cellcolor{green1}RTSR (ours) \cellcolor{green1}34.464 \cellcolor{green1}0.9567 \cellcolor{green1}49.829 \cellcolor{green1}0.063 \cellcolor{green1}1.16 \cellcolor{green1}2.11 (3090) \end{tabular} \label{tab:challengeresult}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{minipage}{0.3\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[clip, viewport=120 120 600 450, width=.98\linewidth]{visual/Chime_3x_HR.jpg}} \text{HD HR} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[clip, viewport=120 120 600 450, width=.98\linewidth]{visual/Chime_3x_Lan.jpg}} \text{HD Lanczos} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[clip, viewport=120 120 600 450, width=.98\linewidth]{visual/Chime_3x_SR.jpg}} \text{HD SR} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[clip, viewport=120 150 600 480, width=.98\linewidth]{visual/Chime_4x_HR.jpg}} \text{4K HR} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[clip, viewport=120 150 600 480, width=.98\linewidth]{visual/Chime_4x_Lan.jpg}} \text{4K Lanczos} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[clip, viewport=120 150 600 480, width=.98\linewidth]{visual/Chime_4x_SR.jpg}} \text{4K SR} \end{minipage} \caption{Visual comparison: The Chimera-WindAndNature sequence is downsampled and compressed using CRF=55. The ground truth, Lanczos filter upsampling, and RTSR recovery results are shown for comparison.} \label{fig:visual}
elements.sent-2411-075: Table \ref{tab:challengeresult summarizes the results of the proposed RTSR method alongside the other five models submitted to the AIM 2024 Grand Challenge \cite{conde2024aim in terms of the average PSNR-Y, SSIM-Y and VMAF values for all test sequences and rate points (QPs).}}
elements.sent-2411-076: It can be observed that RTSR offers the best PSNR-Y performance among all six models, with up to a 0.5dB gain over the second best (in Track 1).
elements.sent-2411-077: Although its VMAF performance is relatively lower when directly compared to SuperBicubic++ and FSMD, RTSR provides the best overall trade-off between complexity and performance, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:radar-chart , where the point corresponding to RTSR is beyond the Pareto front of the other five models in three out of four cases.}
elements.sent-2411-078: It is noted that while these metrics have been commonly used to measure compression performance, they are not designed or optimized for super-resolved (in particular AI-based super-resolved) content.
elements.sent-2411-079: Their reliability for this type of content has previously been reported to be lower than that for compressed content \cite{zhou2024database,mackin2018study,mackin2018srqm .}
elements.sent-2411-080: It can also be observed that the processing speed of RTSR is 0.8 ms per frame for the $\times 3$ up-scaling and 2 ms for $\times 4$, which demonstrates the model's ability to handle high-resolution up-scaling tasks with impressive efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-081: Figure \ref{fig:visual presents a visual comparison between RTSR and the Lanczos5 filter (the anchor in the challenge)\footnote{We are unable to obtain results generated by other submitted models.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: It can be seen that RTSR effectively preserves fine details with enhanced visual quality.
elements.sent-2411-083: It not only reduces visible artifacts but also offers a more natural, visually pleasing output, which closely aligns with the original high-resolution sequence.
elements.sent-2411-084: This demonstrates the superior perceptual performance of RTSR compared to traditional upsampling methods.
elements.sent-2411-085: In this paper, a low-complexity super-resolution method, RTSR, is presented, which has been designed to upscale 360p SVT-AV1 compressed videos to 1080p and 540p to 4K.
elements.sent-2411-086: The CNN-based network used was specifically optimized for AV1 compressed content using a dual-teacher knowledge distillation training strategy, which enhanced the overall reconstruction performance.
elements.sent-2411-087: The results show its excellent super-resolution performance across diverse content alongside a good trade-off between complexity and performance.
elements.sent-2411-088: Future work should focus on further improving the performance of the model and its application to other coding standards.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
